items.mistake: Treating a 2D contour plot of two weights as the entire neural network landscape
items.fix: Label plots explicitly as slices or projections; reason about the full n-dimensional surface separately.
items.why: Millions of other parameters are fixed or absent in the plot; geometry in the slice need not represent the basin the full model occupies.
items.example: A contour plot of (w₁, w₂) with all other layers frozen shows a local patch, not the global landscape of ResNet-50.

items.mistake: Assuming every point with zero gradient is a good stopping point
items.fix: Classify critical points: check curvature (Hessian eigenvalues in theory, loss increase under small perturbations in practice) to distinguish minima from saddles.
items.why: Saddle points satisfy ∇L = 0 but are not minima; stopping there leaves you at a precarious plateau, not a valley floor.
items.example: If loss drops when you add ε·u for random direction u, you are likely on a saddle or sharp ridge, not a robust minimum.

items.mistake: Equating lowest training loss on the surface with best model
items.fix: Evaluate validation/test metrics and consider flatness or regularization; compare multiple runs and checkpoints.
items.why: The deepest training valley can be sharp and memorize noise; a slightly higher training loss in a flatter basin may generalize better.
items.example: Two checkpoints: L_train = 0.05 vs 0.08 but L_val = 0.9 vs 0.6—the second is often preferable despite higher training loss.
